texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thin (2-7 m m) polycrystalline randomly-oriented zeolite MFI membranes were prepared on an a -Al2O3 support by one or more subsequent hydrothermal treatments. Different particle sizes (275-700 nm) in the layer were achieved by changing the synthesis temperature (371-459 K). Zeolite membranes, prepared by two subsequent hydrothermal treatments have an optimum quality. Membranes prepared by a single hydrothermal treatment have defects and zeolite membranes obtained by three or more hydrothermal treatments become too thick and crack during the removal of the template. The zeolite MFI membranes were characterised by permeation measurements using single gases and mixtures of n -butane ( n -C4H10) and isobutane ( i -C4H10). Permselectivities, F a , and separation factors, , for n -C4H10/ i -C4H10 decrease strongly with an increasing number of defects and hence provide a good indication of membrane quality. The separation, on the other hand, of a CH4/ n -C4H10 mixture at high occupation of n -C4H10 is not much affected by small defects. This is ascribed to blocking of those defects by capillary condensation of the butane. Flux values decrease linearly with increasing zeolite crystallite size in the top layer indicating that the effective membrane thickness is only one particle thick. During hydrothermal treatment zeolite particles also grow at the other side of the support but do not decrease significantly the flux through the supported zeolite membrane. # 1998 Elsevier Science B.V.

texts.orig: [12,14]. The performance of these zeolite-filled polymeric membranes was improved compared to the 'unfilled' membranes. This result suggested that a 'pure' zeolite membrane can perform even better. The first patent on the preparation of supported zeolite membranes was by Suzuki in 1987 [1]. He claimed that ultra-thin (1 nm) zeolite (A, X, Y and ZSM-5) membranes could be prepared on porous films (ceramic, vycor and polymer). Geus et al. [2] showed that zeolite particles could be embedded in an inorganic matrix by sol-gel and CVI techniques. These methods of preparation, however, were rather complicated and the membranes obtained had many defects. Nowadays, hydrothermal synthesis is generally used for the preparation of zeolite MFI [4-11,13,14], zeolite A [18] and ferrierite [19] layers on supports. Sano et al. [9,10] reported the preparation of zeolite MFI layers on PTFE, filter paper and stainless steel. Tsikoyiannis and Haag [6,7] described the preparation of an unsupported continuous intergrown film of ZSM-5 crystals with a thickness of 200 m m. A high selectivity, 17, for n -hexane (C6H14)/2,2-dimethylbutane (DMB) was found for this membrane. Geus et al. and Bakker et al. prepared a 40-50 m mthick zeolite MFI layer on a clay and a stainless steel support [5], respectively. Bakker et al. [5] found 30 for the mixture of n -C4H10/ i -C4H10. Jia et al. [12,13] prepared a zeolite MFI membrane on a -Al2O3 support by using a similar (hydrothermal) preparation method but found a low 3-6 for n -C4H10/ i -C4H10 at room temperature. Typical fluxes and selectivities of zeolite MFI, obtained by a number of groups are summarised in Table 1. These data suggest that it is difficult to prepare 'defect-free' (high quality) zeolite membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Variation of particle size is important to study intergrain transport phenomena such as surface-transfer limitations and easy diffusion paths along grain boundaries. Variation of layer thickness is important to obtain the minimum defect-free thickness possible and the formation of cracks due to storage of elastic energy in thicker layers. The membranes are characterised by SEM, thin-layer XRD, light microscopy and permeation measurements. The influence of the synthesis temperature and the number of hydrothermal treatments is shown on transport properties.

This support was made by uniaxial floating die pressing of alumina powder at 100 MPa into a green disk with a thickness of 2.7 mm. texts.orig: The zeolite synthesis solution was prepared by mixing sodium hydroxide (Merck, >99%) and tetrapropyl ammonium hydroxide (Fluka, 20% in water) at room temperature. After a clear solution was obtained, silica powder (Baker, 99.75%) was added to the solution [21]. The silica was dissolved in the solution at 373 K in 300 s. The synthesis solution obtained was cooled down to room temperature in 1.5 h. Water was added to compensate for the weight loss during the preparation of the solution [21]. The molar ratio of the standard zeolite synthesis solution was 100 SiO2: 15 (TPA)2O:5.3 Na2O:1420 H2O. The zeolite synthesis solution was 'aged' for an additional 1.5 h at room temperature.
texts.text: The zeolite synthesis solution was prepared by mixing sodium hydroxide (Merck, >99%) and tetrapropyl ammonium hydroxide (Fluka, 20% in water) at room temperature. After a clear solution was obtained, silica powder (Baker, 99.75%) was added to the solution [21]. The silica was dissolved in the solution at 373 K in 300 s. The synthesis solution obtained was cooled down to room temperature in 1.5 h. Water was added to compensate for the weight loss during the preparation of the solution [21]. The molar ratio of the standard zeolite synthesis solution was 100 SiO2: 15 (TPA)2O:5.3 Na2O:1420 H2O. The zeolite synthesis solution was 'aged' for an additional 1.5 h at room temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The hydrothermal treatments to grow zeolite membranes on the support were performed in a stainless steel autoclave (volume 3.43 10 4 m 3 ). The dry supports were positioned on the bottom of the empty autoclave and 100 g 'aged' zeolite synthesis solution was carefully poured into the autoclave along the supports. The autoclave was closed and heated under autogenous pressure at 371-458 K for 0.7-6 days. After the hydrothermal treatment the composite membrane was washed seven times with water. The temperature of the water used in the washing procedure was 330-355 K [21]. After the washing procedure, the composite membrane was kept wet in water for 40 min. The whole procedure was repeated once and possibly one or two times more for the threeand four-step ISC membranes (P3 and P4), respectively. The composite membranes were dried in a laminar flow cupboard at 303 K for 16-40 h, the R.H. was not controlled. After drying the membrane was checked for cracks/defects by visible light microscopy and permeation measurements. The template in the zeolite pores was removed by heating in air at 823 K for 16 h with a heating rate of 10 K/h and a cooling rate of 20 K/h. The prepared membranes, their codes and preparation conditions are summarised in Table 2.

texts.orig: The permselectivity F a of n -C4H10/ i -C4H10 and the i -C4H10 single gas flux were chosen as an indication of the quality (number of defects) of the membrane. The kinetic diameter, d k , of i -C4H10 (0.5 nm) is near the pore-size of zeolite MFI (0.55 nm). The d k of n -C4H10 is significantly smaller (0.4 nm) and hence n -C4H10 has a much higher diffusion mobility than i -C4H10. The test T was chosen such that no blocking of defects by capillary condensation was possible. Both n -C4H10 and i -C4H10 are strongly adsorbing gases so that the value of their defect-free membrane F a is governed by diffusion mobilities that are strongly related to d k [23]. If F a at 100 kPa for n -C4H10 and i -C4H10 was >10 at 473 K, the membrane was called a 'defect-free' (high quality) membrane. The CH4 flux was measured for all membranes. At 298 K, a feed pressure of 100 kPa and a permeate pressure of 0 kPa, the CH4 flux through the a -Al2O3 support is about 230 mmol m 2 s 1 [24]. This value is at least two orders of magnitude higher than for the good quality supported zeolite MFI membranes presented here (Tables 4-6). This suggests that the resistance of the support can be neglected compared to the resistance of the zeolite MFI layer.

This results for n -C4H10 or i -C4H10 in Knudsen fluxes of 4.1 mmol m 2 s 1 at 473 K. The measured (total) fluxes of n -C4H10 and i -C4H10 are 13 and 4.8 mmol m 2 s 1 , respectively. texts.orig: Membranes P3 and P4 were gas-tight before removal of the templates, indicating that no defects were present at this stage. After the removal of the templates the MFI layers obviously contained defects because F a for the 100 kPa n -C4H10/100 kPa i -C4H10 mixture was only 1.8 for membrane P3 and even lower for membrane P4 (1.0). Crack defects appear if the top layer is more than about 4 m m thick because in that case it can no longer withstand the stresses developing in the MFI layer during the heat treatment/removal of the template. This phenomenon can be explained by the supposition that, below a certain critical thickness, the elastic energy released by cracking a thin film is too small to balance the energy required to form a crack [25].

texts.orig: The fact that J is found to decrease with increasing synthesis temperature can partly be explained by an SEM thickness, increasing from 3.5-5.5 m m. (In [23] it is shown that with bulk diffusion control, some quite general assumptions and in a homogeneous membrane, J decreases linearly with L ). It is found, however, that the CH 4 flux corrected for the layer thickness ( J L ) still decreases with increasing synthesis temperature. This leads us to the tentative conclusion that the presence of interfaces seems to be beneficial for the flux. This, in turn, is supported by the observation that J A strongly increases with the estimated number of interfaces, A . Especially under growing conditions which promote non-faceted crystal habits or incomplete crystallisation, 'high mobility' grain boundaries between crystal surfaces can occur, providing shortrange transport shortcuts. From the fact that, according to Table 4, selectivity by size exclusion, is observed we may conclude that the open grain boundaries do not form a connected path throughout the membrane structure. The fact that J d is nearly constant supports this and leads us to the tentative conclusion that the effective membrane thickness is only one grain thick.

texts.orig: In membranes prepared by a single hydrothermal treatment small defects are usually present because not all particles grow together.
texts.text: In membranes prepared by a single hydrothermal treatment small defects are usually present because not all particles grow together.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Three or more subsequent hydrothermal treatments yield a low quality membrane, because the layers become too thick (>4 m m) and crack during the removal of the template.
texts.text: Three or more subsequent hydrothermal treatments yield a low quality membrane, because the layers become too thick (>4 m m) and crack during the removal of the template.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The largest flux values for CH4, n -C4H10 and i -C4H10 are measured for membranes prepared at the lowest synthesis temperature and hence smallest particle size. The flux values decrease with increasing synthesis temperature. This effect can be correlated with the increase of the particle size with increasing synthesis temperature and the assumption that the effective membrane thickness is only one particle thick.
texts.text: The largest flux values for CH4, n -C4H10 and i -C4H10 are measured for membranes prepared at the lowest synthesis temperature and hence smallest particle size. The flux values decrease with increasing synthesis temperature. This effect can be correlated with the increase of the particle size with increasing synthesis temperature and the assumption that the effective membrane thickness is only one particle thick.
